data.title: Trump will 'absolutely' consider NATO exit if members don't 'pay their bills'
data.description:
President-elect Donald Trump would "absolutely" consider leaving NATO if members of the alliance didn't "pay their bills," he said in his first post-election network TV interview with NBC.

"NATO has taken advantage of us on trade… They don't take our cars, they don't take our food product, they don't take anything," he noted. "On top of that, we defend them. So it's a double whammy."

"If they're paying their bills, if I think they're treating us fairly, then absolutely I would stay with NATO," Trump declared.

When asked if Ukraine should prepare for less aid from the U.S. after he's sworn in, Trump said, "Possibly. Probably."

"We're in for $350B. And Europe is in for $100B. Why isn't Europe in for the same as us?" he asked, adding that war with Russia is more important for Europe than it is for the U.S.

Trump declined to say if he had spoken to Russian President Vladimir Putin since the election, saying he didn't want to do anything that could impede the negotiation.

On his social media platform Truth Social, Trump claimed Ukraine President Volodymyr Zelenskyy would like to make a deal and "stop the madness."

"There should be an immediate ceasefire and negotiations should begin," Trump posted on Sunday, a day after meeting French and Ukrainian leaders in Paris ahead of the Notre Dame Cathedral's reopening.

Notably, Trump advisers floated proposals to end the war that include Ukraine ceding territory to Russia and taking Kyiv's request to join NATO off the table, Reuters reported last week.

More on Trump foreign policy

  • Trump says U.S. should not get involved in conflict in Syria
  • NATO chief to Trump: Unfavorable Ukraine peace deal would be 'dire threat' to U.S.
  • 'Tariff' is one of Trump's favorite words. What will Mexico and China say?

data.title: Biggest stock movers Monday: SMCI, MOMO, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged lower Monday morning as investors eye this week’s key inflation report. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) data will likely shape expectations for Federal Reserve policy and the U.S. economic outlook.

Here are some of Monday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Hello Group (MOMO) shares rose as much as 7% after the company reported better-than-expected FQ3 results, despite a 12% Y/Y drop in revenue. Looking ahead to FQ4, the company projects total net revenues between RMB2.56B and RMB2.66B, representing a Y/Y decline of 14.7% to 11.4%.
  • Affimed (AFMD) shares surged 8% after the company presented promising data from the LuminICE-203 phase 2 trial at the 66th ASH Annual Meeting. The study, which evaluates the combination of acimtamig (AFM13) and Artiva Biotherapeutics’ allogeneic NK cell product AlloNK® in patients with relapsed/refractory classic Hodgkin lymphoma (R/R cHL), showed a high overall response rate (ORR) of 86% and a complete response rate of 55%. Despite patients being heavily pretreated, the combination therapy demonstrated a well-managed safety profile, with no cases of graft-versus-host disease (GvHD) or neurotoxicity. These results align with previous data from the single-center AFM13-104 study, reinforcing the potential of this combination as an effective treatment option for refractory patients.
  • Shares of Super Micro Computer (SMCI) surged as much as 9% after the company announced that Nasdaq had granted it more time to comply with listing rules. The extension reduces the immediate risk of delisting, as Nasdaq has given Super Micro until February 25 to file its financial report for the fiscal year ended June 30, along with its quarterly report for the period ending September 30. This move provides the company with the breathing room needed to address its reporting delays and maintain its Nasdaq listing.

Biggest stock losers

  • Shares of BioAge (BIOA) plunged 69% after the company announced Friday it would discontinue its Phase 2 study of the weight-loss drug azelaprag due to safety concerns. The trial, which was testing azelaprag both as a monotherapy and in combination with Eli Lilly's tirzepatide (Mounjaro, Zepbound), was halted after liver transaminitis was observed in a few patients. Of the 204 patients enrolled, 11 in the azelaprag groups showed elevated transaminase levels without clinically significant symptoms. As a result, BioAge will cease dosing and stop enrolling new patients in the trial. The company has informed the FDA of its decision and plans to provide updated information on the drug in Q1 2025.

data.title: Cardinal Health awarded $9B Defense Logistics Agency contract modification
data.description:
• Cardinal Health 200 LLC, a business unit of Cardinal Health (NYSE:CAH) has been awarded a maximum $9 billion modification exercising the third 30-month option period of a 30-month base contract, with three 30-month option periods.
  • The contract is to support worldwide ordering and distribution of consumable, commercial, brand name and generic medical surgical supplies in support of the medical surgical prime vendor Generation V program.
  • The project locations are throughout the continental U.S., with a June 6, 2027, performance completion date.
  • The end users of the contract are Air Force, Army, Navy, Marine Corps, Coast Guard and federal civilian agencies.
  • The contracting activity is the Defense Logistics Agency Troop Support, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

data.title: Owens & Minor secures $4.5B Defense Logistics Agency contract modification
data.description:
• Owens and Minor Distribution Inc., a unit of Owens & Minor (NYSE:OMI) has been awarded a maximum $4.5B modification exercising the third 30-month option period of a 30-month base contract with three 30-month option periods.
  • The contract is for worldwide ordering and distribution of consumable, commercial, brand name and generic medical surgical supplies in support of the medical surgical prime vendor Generation V program.
  • Locations of performance are throughout the continental U.S., with a June 6, 2027, performance completion date.
  • The end users of the project are Air Force, Army, Navy, Marine Corps, Coast Guard and federal civilian agencies.
  • The contracting activity is the Defense Logistics Agency Troop Support, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

data.title: Memory chipmaker Kioxia prices IPO in Japan, raising $800M - report
data.description:
Private equity firm Bain Capital-backed Kioxia raised about 120B yen (around $800M), including an overallotment, in its IPO in Japan, Reuters reported.

Kioxia, which is among the world's largest makers of NAND flash memory, had priced its IPO at 1,455 yen per share. This values the company at about 784B yen (around $5.21B), the report added.

Kioxia had set an indicative price range of 1,390 yen to 1,520 yen per share last month.

Kioxia is slated to list on the Tokyo Stock Exchange on Dec. 18.

Bain, which led a consortium to acquire Kioxia from Toshiba for $18B in 2018, has thought about listing Kioxia in the past as well. A 56% stake in Kioxia is owned by a special-purpose company formed by Bain and South Korean chipmaker SK hynix. Toshiba has a 41% stake in Kioxia, according to a previous report by Nikkei.

Toshiba and Bain will sell shares as part of the IPO, while separately, Kioxia will issue new stock, raising 31B yen, the report noted.

Kioxia, reportedly, had called off IPO plans for October in Japan, after investors pushed Bain to almost half the valuation it was seeking for the chipmaker. Previously, Bain was in discussions with investment banks to list the Japanese company. Kioxia was expected to raise about $500M, and garner a market capitalization of over 1.5T yen ($10.3B) via the IPO.

The Tokyo-based company was also in discussions for a transaction to integrate with Western Digital's (NASDAQ:WDC) memory business, intending to form an entity that could challenge NAND memory market heavyweight Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). However, discussions were called off in October 2023. SK hynix had said that it would not agree to the merger of Kioxia and Western Digital's flash memory business due to impact on the value of the company's investment.

data.title: US Foods awarded $130.4M Defense Logistics Agency contract
data.description:
• US Foods (NYSE:USFD) has been awarded a maximum $130.4 million contract for full-line food and beverage items.
  • This is a 266-day bridge contract, with the ordering period end date being Aug. 30, 2025.
  • The contracting activity is the Defense Logistics Agency Troop Support, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

data.title: Acer’s November revenue up 20.9% M/M to NT$22.75B as Chromebook sales surges 54%
data.description:
• Acer (OTC:ACEYY) has reported consolidated revenues of NT$22.75B for November, up 20.9% from NT$18.82B in October 2024.

  • Year-to-November revenues totaled NT$240.16B, up 10.4% Y/Y.

  • Revenues from the personal computers and display business grew 11.0% Y/Y in November and 9.4% Y/Y year-to-November.
  • Chromebooks were up 54.1% Y/Y in November, signaling strong demand.
  • Businesses other than computers and displays contributed 26.8% of the group's total revenues in November and 28.3% year-to-November.
  • Bifurcating Acer's public subsidiaries' revenue: Altos Computing Inc. up 3.3% Y/Y in November and 64.4% Y/Y year-to-November; Acer ITS Inc. revenues 59.1% Y/Y in November and 35.3% Y/Y year-to-November.

  • Two subsidiaries, Acerpure and Protrade Applied Materials, began trading on Taipei Exchange Emerging Market, while Winking Studios listed on LSE AIM and SGX Catalyst.

data.title: European markets edged higher as investors weigh geopolitical risks
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.4%

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.1%

France (CAC:IND) +0.7%

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) rose +0.3% on Monday, as investors weighed geopolitical turmoil in the Middle East after the ousting of Syrian President Bashar al-Assad over the weekend. Focus also remains on the European Central Bank, which is expected to cut interest rates again this week.

U.S. stock futures were little changed Sunday night after the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite posted their third straight winning week.

Coming up in the session: A lighter day on charts.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 1 basis points to 4.16%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down less than 1 basis point at 2.11%.

UK's 10-year yield was down less than 1 basis point to 4.28%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (EWG), (GF), (EWI), (EWQ), (FGM), (DAX), (FLGR), (FXB), (EWU), (FKU), (EWUS), (FLGB), (GREK)

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Hello Group tops Q3 estimates; initiates Q4 revenue outlook
Google Payment placed under CFPB supervision, company hits back with lawsuit
data.description:
The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau has established supervisory authority over Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Google Payment, citing "reasonable cause" to determine that its conduct poses risks to consumers. The company immediately sued the CFPB, claiming regulatory overreach.

The watchdog pointed to Google's practices in investigating allegedly erroneous transactions, and its conduct in connection with the prevention of fraudulent and unauthorized transactions.

The CFPB clarified that its order "does not constitute a finding that the entity has engaged in wrongdoing."

In response, Google sued the CFPB, claiming the order would subject it to "burdensome supervision" and is "in excess of statutory authority."

The company previously argued that the designation was only warranted if there is a "meaningful possibility of substantial harm to consumers." It also claimed that the CFPB relied on "33 cherry-picked complaints" involving peer-to-peer payments that it discontinued earlier this year.

The CFPB said it relied on 267 consumer complaints, and the regulatory framework for the designation does not specify the degree of risk needed to justify supervision.

"Further, Google's discontinuation of the Google Pay App and the P2P payment platform is not a basis to refrain from designating Google for supervision," it added.
